TextENGNew: The moment club activities ended, Rena bolted home.
Apparently she'd found something adorable at her usual treasure-hunting spot.
changeReason: Old says 'she found something really ``kyute``'; new says 'she'd found something adorable,' where old uses the stylized 'kyute' to represent Rena's speech quirk while new uses standard 'adorable,' losing the character-specific speech pattern present in the Japanese かぁいい.

TextENGNew: So today, for once, it was just the two of us—Mion and me—walking home together.

"Well, about that treasure-hunting spot of hers."
"...It's basically a garbage heap past those woods over there."
changeReason: Old has one continuous quote; new splits into two separate quoted lines with different speakers/pauses, matching the Japanese @k tag that indicates a text break between the two statements.

TextENGNew: Mion said as much with a resigned shrug. Fair enough—a garbage heap isn't exactly the kind of place a girl her age should be getting starry-eyed over...

"But what kind of treasure could you possibly find in an illegal dumping ground?"
"I just hope she doesn't hurt herself."

"Ahahahaha!"
"She'll be fine, she'll be fine—it's not her first time."
"Besides, that place used to be the dam construction site, so there's all kinds of things lying around."
"All kinds of things... huh..."
changeReason: Old says 'All kinds of things... huh...'; new says 'All kinds of things... huh...' but the actual ENG is '``Lots of things, yeah...``' vs new '"All kinds of things... huh..."' The old uses 'Lots of things, yeah' while new uses 'All kinds of things... huh,' where 賑やか means 'lively/bustling' not 'lots of things'—both miss the mark but differently.

TextENGNew: "Yeah, a lot happened there—battles and all that!@k@vS19/03/660300062. Sit-ins, demonstrations, you name it!"
changeReason: Old says 'Yeah, a whole bunch of stuff happened here. Like this great battle! There were strikes and demonstrations and stuff!'; new splits into two quoted lines and changes 'strikes' to 'Sit-ins' matching 座りこみ (sit-in protest) in the Japanese, which is different from a labor strike.

TextENGNew: "Battles" didn't sound very peaceful, but I decided to hear her out.
changeReason: Old says 'Calling it a ``great battle`` makes it sound kinda violent'; new says '"Battles" didn't sound very peaceful,' where old adds 'kinda violent' interpretation while the Japanese simply says 穏やかじゃない (not peaceful/calm).

TextENGNew: Mion launched into the story of those events, gesturing animatedly as she spoke.

"So they were going to drown Hinamizawa under a lake..."
"That really is unthinkable."
changeReason: Old says 'So they wanted to sink our Hinamizawa into the lake...? You're right, that is awful'; new says 'So they were going to drown Hinamizawa under a lake... That really is unthinkable,' where 'unthinkable' (とんでもない) better matches the Japanese than 'awful.'

TextENGNew:
"The government officials were absolutely one-sided about it."
"Arrogant and high-handed!"
"Once they realized money wouldn't settle the matter, they resorted to every form of harassment they could think of?!"
"Horrible people!"
changeReason: Old says 'The government guys were really adamant about it. Acting all big and full of themselves!'; new says 'The government officials were absolutely one-sided about it. Arrogant and high-handed!' where 'one-sided' (一方的) more accurately translates the Japanese than 'adamant,' and 'high-handed' (威張ってた) is more precise than 'full of themselves.'

TextENGNew: Mion spoke with rising fury, as if the whole thing were unfolding right before her eyes.
changeReason: Old says Mion 'spoke excitedly' but Japanese 声を荒げながら means 'raising her voice angrily/roughly' — wrong emotional register (excitement vs anger), though both are high-energy.

TextENGNew: "I'm amazed you actually won... You were up against the government, weren't you?"

"The village headman and the prominent families petitioned everyone they could reach."
"They went to Tokyo, worked their connections with all sorts of politicians..."
"...And in the end the plan was retracted."
changeReason: Old says 'The mayor and prominent figures in the village all signed petitions'; new says 'The village headman and the prominent families petitioned everyone they could reach,' where 'headman' (村長) is more accurate for the rural village context than 'mayor,' and 'petitioned everyone they could reach' (方々に陳情した) is different from 'signed petitions.'

TextENGNew:
"It was our total victory!"
"Ahahahahahaha!"

"There wasn't any... violence, was there?"
"Like assaults... or murder—"
changeReason: Old says 'There wasn't any... violence, was there? Like assaults... or murders?'; new says 'There wasn't any... violence, was there? Like assaults... or murder—' where old ends with a complete word 'murders?' and new ends with a cut-off 'murder—' matching the Japanese which shows Keiichi being interrupted mid-word (殺人、).

TextENGNew: "There wasn't."
She said it flatly, cutting me off.
A strange, unsettling dissonance.
changeReason: Old says 'An eerie malaise washes over me'; new says 'A strange, unsettling dissonance,' where 'malaise' implies sickness/unease while 'dissonance' (違和感) more precisely translates the Japanese sense of something feeling 'off' or incongruent.

TextENGNew: Her words carried a period—a firm full stop.
changeReason: Old says 'Her words signaled the end of discussion'; new says 'Her words carried a period—a firm full stop,' which more literally translates the Japanese metaphor about a ピリオド (period/full stop) being embedded in her words.

TextENGNew: Did she not want me prying any further? Or perhaps...
Perhaps this wasn't the kind of thing you could just bring up casually and dig into at will.
My unsatisfied curiosity drooped inside my chest, restless and without anywhere to go.
changeReason: Old says 'My curiosity unsated, I sighed dejectedly inside'; new says 'My unsatisfied curiosity drooped inside my chest, restless and without anywhere to go,' where new captures the Japanese imagery of curiosity being 'restless and without a place' (所在なくうなだれていた) more vividly.

TextENGNew:
"Later then—see you tomorrow!"
"Kei-chan, don't wash that off until you're home, okay?!"
"I know, I won't!"
Mion stole glances at my face and struggled to stifle her laughter.
...I wonder just what kind of wretched doodle they drew on me...
You'll regret this, Mion...!
changeReason: Old says 'Damn you, Mion! You shall rue this day...!'; new says 'You'll regret this, Mion...!' where old adds 'Damn you' and 'rue this day' which are more dramatic than the Japanese 覚えてろ魅音め (Remember this, Mion).

TextENGNew: If the tables ever turn, I'll plaster every inch of your face—so thick that not even a scouring brush can remove it...!!!
I arrived home and walked straight into my mother, earning myself yet another round of embarrassment...
...If I could reason with certainty that the left card was not the joker, then I ought to be able to declare with confidence that the right card was.
changeReason: Old says 'The left card definitely wasn't the joker. If I could trust that theory, then I should've been able to declare that the card to the right was the joker'; new says 'If I could reason with certainty that the left card was not the joker, then I ought to be able to declare with confidence that the right card was,' restructuring to match the conditional logic of the Japanese more precisely.

TextENGNew: Even without Mion's goading—...the right was the most suspicious.

But wasn't it too rash to base my conclusion solely on Satoko's reaction?!
More carefully... slowly... Ah!

...I remember now...!!
That mark... the left card... there's no doubt!
The Seven of Clubs!!!

"Mmm..."
"I'm pretty sure that's the Seven of Clubs..."

I've got it!
The left card is the Seven of Clubs!
Which means the remaining right card is... the Joker!!!!
And then—just as my hand was about to touch the right card, I stopped dead.

"...Heh heh heh... heh heh heh..."
"Just what I'd expect from you, Mion."
changeReason: Old says 'I would expect no less from you'; new says 'Just what I'd expect from you, Mion,' adding 'Mion' as the addressee matching the Japanese (さすが魅音だよ).

TextENGNew: The murmurs rippled through the room at my sudden pause—every last person had been certain the right card was the joker.
changeReason: Old says 'There was a wave of murmurs amongst the onlookers at my sudden halt, right before I confirmed the right card was the joker'; new says 'The murmurs rippled through the room at my sudden pause—every last person had been certain the right card was the joker,' where old says 'right before I confirmed' (implying he was about to confirm) while new says 'every last person had been certain' (場の誰もが...確信した) matching the Japanese about everyone's certainty.

TextENGNew:
"?? Huh?"
"What on earth could Keiichi-san possibly be saying??"
"But the left one is..."

"Oh...?"
"What makes you so certain the right one isn't the joker, Kei-chan?"
An unexpected question from Mion.@k Everyone except me was thrown into confusion.
changeReason: Old says 'Mion let loose an unexpected question. Everyone else was baffled'; new says 'An unexpected question from Mion. Everyone except me was thrown into confusion,' where new adds 'except me' (俺を除く) from the Japanese, which is a meaningful detail showing Keiichi wasn't confused.

TextENGNew:
"I have no idea what the right card is."
"But I do know that the left card is the Seven of Clubs."

"Then!"
"Doesn't that leave the right one as the joker?!"
"One of the two has to be the joker—that was the deal, wasn't it?!"

"Yeah."
"...One of the two is the joker—that much is certainly true."
"...Keiichi's intuition is sharp."

"Huh?"
"Rika-chan, what do you mean by...?"

"The thing is—"
"...the Seven of Clubs."
"...That's the card I discarded a moment ago when it paired with Satoko's Seven of Spades...!"
changeReason: Old says 'So basically... the Seven of Clubs... I discarded that by matching it with Satoko's Seven of Spades earlier...!'; new says 'The thing is— ...the Seven of Clubs. ...That's the card I discarded a moment ago when it paired with Satoko's Seven of Spades...!' where old starts with 'So basically' (つまりさ) while new uses 'The thing is—', both acceptable, but the restructuring better matches the dramatic pause structure of the Japanese.

TextENGNew: Everyone's gaze snapped to the discard pile in unison!
In that chaotic jumble of discarded cards, there was no way to verify the truth...!
changeReason: Old says 'The truth of the matter couldn't be discerned from that sloppy pile'; new says 'In that chaotic jumble of discarded cards, there was no way to verify the truth,' where 'chaotic jumble' (ぐちゃぐちゃ) is more vivid than 'sloppy pile' and better matches the Japanese.

TextENGNew:
"In other words—"
"...Mion picked up one card from the discard pile... and layered it over the left card... which means...!!!"
changeReason: Old says 'Mion pocketed one of discarded cards... then aligned it over the left card'; new says 'Mion picked up one card from the discard pile... and layered it over the left card,' where 'picked up from the discard pile' (捨て札を１枚拾って) is more specific about the source than 'pocketed.'

TextENGNew:
"...Oh! I understand!"
"The left card is being disguised as the Seven of Clubs, isn't it?!"
For the first time since the game began, a shadow crossed Mion's face—and I didn't miss it.
And then... with a theatrical flourish and a twist, I made my decisive declaration!!
"The joker is... the LEFT haaaaaaaand!!!!!"
changeReason: Old says 'The joker is... ...the left ooooooooooone!!!!!!'; new says 'The joker is... the LEFT haaaaaaaand!!!!!' where old says 'one' and new says 'hand' (左手), matching the Japanese which specifically says 'left hand' (左手だぁあぁああああぁああッッ).

TextENGNew:
A searing, white-hot instant...!
That one second felt like hours to everyone in the room.
changeReason: Old says 'It was an intense moment...! That one second felt like an eternity for everyone there'; new says 'A searing, white-hot instant...! That one second felt like hours to everyone in the room,' where 'hours' (何時間にも) is more specific than 'eternity,' and 'searing, white-hot' (熱過ぎる) better captures the Japanese imagery.

TextENGNew: Into that silence, a quiet 'heh...' escaped Mion's lips as she broke into a defiant smirk.
changeReason: Old says 'Amidst the silence, a smirk flashes across Mion's face'; new says 'Into that silence, a quiet "heh..." escaped Mion's lips as she broke into a defiant smirk,' adding the vocalization 'heh' (ふっ……) and 'defiant' (不敵な) from the Japanese.

TextENGNew: "Just as I'd expect, Kei-cha—"

"—or so it might seem, but in fact it's the right hand."

TextENGNew: "WHA?!"
With Mion standing there dumbfounded, I reached out and plucked the card from her right hand.

TextENGNew: A chorus of shocked "Gya?!" sounds burst from the other three.

TextENGNew: That card was—as it turned out—the joker.
"Wh... K-Kei-chan...?!"

TextENGNew: ...But wait. The other three looked equally pale... Why?

TextENGNew: "Y-You all knew?!"
All three of them let out a quiet, knowing laugh.

TextENGNew: I will state for the record that a cold shiver ran down my spine.

TextENGNew: But looking over, I saw Mion staring intently at her cards with a furrowed brow. Then, slowly, she raised her gaze.
"Kei-chan, how did you figure it out...?"
Everyone looked at me at once. It was plain that Mion's words gave voice to the question on all their minds.

"Simple, really."
"Knowing that I might recognize a card by its marks, you still chose to show a distinctively identifiable card in your left hand..."
As I said that, I glanced briefly at Satoko.
"Huh... are you saying I did something?"

"Perfectly transparent... Yes!"
"Satoko made a slip of the tongue...!"
changeReason: Old says 'You're so transparent... That's right!'; new says 'Perfectly transparent... Yes!' where old adds 'you're' making it about a person, while Japanese says 実に見え見えな (utterly obvious/transparent) describing the situation, not directly addressing someone.

TextENGNew:
"M-Me...?"
"What slip of the tongue are you claiming I made?!"
Without missing a beat, I mimicked Satoko's manner of speech and said:
"Doesn't that leave the right card as the joker?!"

"You overplayed your hand, Satoko."
"There's no way you would ever volunteer that kind of information to me!"
changeReason: Old says 'You got carried away, Satoko'; new says 'You overplayed your hand, Satoko,' where 策に溺れた means 'drowned in one's own stratagem/overplayed one's hand' which is more strategic in meaning than 'got carried away.'

TextENGNew: Satoko's face went pale in an instant, and she began to tremble.
"And then there's you, Mion...!"

"Your reaction was far too uncharacteristically honest for you..."
"The combined effect of those two pieces of misdirection was designed to push me into overthinking—that was your whole plan."
changeReason: Old says 'The way you reacted to me was too uncharacteristically honest...The synergy of your two manipulations left me to conclude none other that your ultimate goal was to make me overthink things'; new says 'Your reaction was far too uncharacteristically honest for you...The combined effect of those two pieces of misdirection was designed to push me into overthinking—that was your whole plan,' where new uses 'misdirection' for ミスリード (mislead) rather than 'manipulations,' and is clearer overall.

TextENGNew: "B-But what if she had stacked two cards on top of each other, like we said earlier...?"
I wagged my index finger at her.

TextENGNew:
"If she had stacked two cards, even if it fooled me, I could always accuse her of cheating and wriggle off the hook. So that strategy was off the table."
"—Am I wrong, Mion?!"
changeReason: Old says 'If you were to stack two cards, you would give me the easy excuse of just saying, "Oh, you cheated." TextENGNew: At that, Mion—who had been listening in silence—suddenly let out a muffled chuckle, which quickly swelled into a full, uninhibited laugh.

"I concede...!"

TextENGNew:

TextENGNew: Still... marking up a girl's face with a permanent marker really did seem a bit much...

TextENGNew:
